1.synthesis: After completing his discourse in the hearing of the people, Jesus enters Capernaum (v.1). A centurion has a beloved servant who is ill and at the point of death. He sends Jewish elders to Jesus, asking him to come and heal the servant; the elders plead on his behalf: "He is worthy to have you do this for him, for he loves our nation and has built our synagogue" (vv.2–5). When Jesus is not far from the house, the centurion sends friends with the message: "Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy to have you come under my roof. Therefore I did not presume to come to you myself. But say the word, and let my servant be healed. For I too am a man set under authority, with soldiers under me" (vv.6–8). Jesus marvels and turns to the crowd: "I tell you, not even in Israel have I found such faith" (v.9). When the messengers return, they find the servant well (v.10).
1.historical_context: The centurion commanded approximately 80 soldiers, typically non-Jewish in Galilee (possibly Syrian or Idumaean auxiliaries under Herod Antipas, since there was no direct Roman garrison in Galilee at this time). His benefaction — building the Capernaum synagogue — is archaeologically interesting: a first-century synagogue was excavated at Capernaum, though its exact relation to this centurion is uncertain. Luke's version differs from Matthew's (Matthew 8:5–13) by introducing intermediaries (Jewish elders, then friends) rather than the centurion coming himself — a detail that enhances the centurion's humility (he does not presume to approach Jesus directly). The military metaphor for authority (v.8: I command and it is done; therefore you can command and it will be done) reflects Roman military culture: orders within a chain of command are absolute.
1.literary_structure: The intercession structure is Lukan: it is the Jewish elders, not the centurion himself, who first approach Jesus; the centurion speaks through friends. This creates a distance-healing that mirrors the theological point the centurion makes: Jesus does not need to come; the word alone is sufficient, just as the centurion's orders are obeyed without his physical presence. Jesus's marvel (v.9) is one of only two places in the Gospels where Jesus is described as astonished (the other being Mark 6:6 — astonished at unbelief in Nazareth). The centurion's faith produces the opposite of Nazareth: maximum faith from a Gentile outsider where minimum faith characterized the home synagogue.
1.theological_application: "Not even in Israel have I found such faith" (v.9). The centurion's faith is characterized by two elements: an understanding of authority (he knows what chain-of-command obedience looks like and applies it to Jesus's word over disease) and an understanding of his own unworthiness to receive Jesus ("I am not worthy to have you come under my roof," v.6). True faith combines these: confidence in the Lord's authority and humility about one's own claim on it. The centurion does not argue his merits (though the elders do) but throws himself entirely on the word of the one who commands. This is the faith-pattern that Luke's Gospel consistently holds up: trust in the power and grace of Jesus rather than confidence in one's religious standing.
1.christology: The centurion's analogy — "I too am a man set under authority, with soldiers under me" (v.8) — implicitly places Jesus above all earthly authority chains: the centurion is set under the authority of Rome, but Jesus is set under no human authority; his command over disease flows from a different order of authority entirely. The centurion grasps what Israel's religious leadership has been slow to perceive: that the word of Jesus operates with divine effectiveness over the created order and its disorders. His faith is essentially an understanding of who Jesus is — one whose word carries divine executive power — and a willingness to trust that word for his servant's healing.

11.synthesis: Soon afterward Jesus goes to a town called Nain with his disciples and a great crowd. As he approaches the gate of the town, a dead man is being carried out — the only son of his mother, who is a widow. A large crowd from the town accompanies her (vv.11–12). Jesus sees her and has compassion on her: "Do not weep." He comes up and touches the bier; the bearers stand still. "Young man, I say to you, arise." The dead man sits up and begins to speak; Jesus gives him to his mother (vv.13–15). Fear seizes them all and they glorify God: "A great prophet has risen among us!" and "God has visited his people!" (v.16). The account is unique to Luke — the only resurrection of a stranger's dead in the Synoptics, and the only one triggered entirely by Jesus's compassion without being asked.
11.historical_context: Nain ("pleasant") is a village in the Jezreel Valley, about 25 miles from Capernaum. A widow who loses her only son lost her entire social safety net in the ancient world: no husband, no male heir, no economic protection. Her condition is the extreme of the social vulnerability that runs through Luke's concern for the poor and marginalized. The proximity of Nain to Shunem (about 3 miles) invites the intertextual echo with 2 Kings 4:8–37, where Elisha raised a widow's son at Shunem — and with 1 Kings 17:17–24, where Elijah raised the widow of Zarephath's son (the same widow Jesus had cited in the Nazareth sermon as the type of God's grace to Gentiles). The crowd's acclamation, "A great prophet has risen among us" (v.16), identifies Jesus with the Elijah-Elisha prophet category but does not yet transcend it.
11.literary_structure: The Nain raising is the third in a sequence of escalating miracles in Luke 7 that lead to John's question (vv.18–35): centurion's servant healed at a distance (power over illness) → widow's son raised from death (power over death) → John's query about whether Jesus is the one to come. The answer to John's question (v.22) includes "the dead are raised" as one of the signs — the Nain raising is the demonstration of this claim. Luke introduces Jesus's emotion here with the word esplanchnisthē ("he had compassion") — the visceral gut-feeling of compassion that is used of Jesus in all three Synoptics but appears here in a context where nobody has asked him for anything. Compassion precedes the request; grace precedes the petition.
11.theological_application: "Do not weep" (v.13) — spoken to the mother before the resurrection, when there is no visible reason to stop weeping. Jesus speaks the command before the evidence that makes it rational. This is the pattern of kingdom hope: the command to stop mourning is grounded in the one who speaks it, not in the circumstances that surround it. The raising of the widow's son is not merely a miracle of power but an act of divine compassion for a woman in the worst situation her world could produce. "God has visited his people" (v.16) echoes the language of Exodus and the Psalms: the God who visits Israel in her oppression (Exodus 3:16) is visiting now in the person of Jesus.
11.christology: "Young man, I say to you, arise" (v.14). Elijah and Elisha raised the dead through extended prayer and physical contact; Jesus raises the dead with an authoritative word, as creator-Lord of life. The crowd identifies a prophet; Luke's narrative is working toward the disclosure that Jesus exceeds the prophet category — he is the one about whom the prophets spoke, not merely one more prophet in their line. The giving of the son back to his mother (v.15) echoes 1 Kings 17:23 almost exactly: what Elijah did through prayer, Jesus does with a command. The one who speaks is the Lord of life who speaks the resurrection into existence wherever he addresses the dead.

18.synthesis: John's disciples report all these things to him in prison. John calls two and sends them to the Lord: "Are you the one who is to come, or shall we look for another?" (vv.18–20). In that hour Jesus heals many of diseases, plagues, evil spirits, and gives sight to the blind; he answers: "Go and tell John what you have seen and heard: the blind receive their sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news preached to them. And blessed is the one who is not offended by me" (vv.21–23). Jesus then speaks to the crowds about John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to see?... A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and more than a prophet... he is Elijah who is to come" (vv.24–28). The rejection of both John and Jesus by this generation is like children in a marketplace: nothing satisfies them — neither John's asceticism nor Jesus's feasting (vv.29–35).
18.historical_context: John is imprisoned by Herod Antipas, according to Luke's narrative frame already noted at 3:19–20 (and Josephus, Ant. 18.5.2, who confirms the imprisonment at Machaerus fortress). John's question from prison — "Are you the one who is to come?" (ho erchomenos) — is the technical eschatological expression for the expected Messianic figure. Jesus's answer assembles quotations from Isaiah 35:5–6 (blind see, deaf hear, lame walk), Isaiah 61:1 (poor have good news), and Isaiah 26:19 (dead raised) — he answers by pointing to the fulfilled prophecy rather than making a direct messianic self-claim. The marketplace parable (vv.31–35) describes a generation that played neither wedding nor funeral — neither John's ascetic style nor Jesus's celebratory fellowship elicited a response of repentance.
18.literary_structure: The section has three movements: John's question and Jesus's answer (vv.18–23) → Jesus's testimony about John (vv.24–28) → this generation's rejection of both (vv.29–35). The first is christological (what is Jesus?); the second is eschatological (what was John?); the third is diagnostic (what is the problem?). Luke alone preserves v.29–30 (the contrast between tax collectors who received John's baptism and the Pharisees/lawyers who rejected it), making the rejection of wisdom's children (v.35) concrete in the narrative's social terms: the poor and sinners received John and will receive Jesus; the religious elite rejected both.
18.theological_application: "Blessed is the one who is not offended by me" (v.23). The beatitude is addressed to John in prison, to disciples who might stumble at the gap between John's preaching of fire-and-judgment and Jesus's ministry of mercy-and-feast, and to all who face the scandal of a Messiah who does not match their expectations. Jesus's answer to John's question does not say "yes, I am the one" but demonstrates it — the Isaiah-shaped signs are happening, and John must calibrate his expectations by what Scripture said, not by what he assumed. "Wisdom is justified by all her children" (v.35): the proof of God's wisdom in sending both John and Jesus is found in the lives of those who received them.
18.christology: Jesus's answer to John assembles a mosaic of Isaiah passages that describe the eschatological restoration of creation — blind see, deaf hear, lame walk, dead rise, poor hear good news. Each category of healing that Jesus lists is simultaneously a proof of his identity and a description of what the kingdom's arrival looks like. He is "the one who is to come" because the signs that Isaiah attached to God's final saving act are happening through him. The addition of "the dead are raised" (not in Isaiah 35 or 61 directly) reflects the Nain resurrection just narrated — the healing of death itself is the most encompassing sign of the kingdom's arrival, and it is added to the scriptural list because it has just been demonstrated in history.

36.synthesis: A Pharisee named Simon invites Jesus to eat with him. A woman in the city who is a sinner, learning that Jesus is reclining in the Pharisee's house, brings an alabaster flask of ointment, stands behind his feet weeping, and begins to wet his feet with her tears, wiping them with her hair, kissing them, anointing them with ointment (vv.36–38). Simon thinks: "If this man were a prophet, he would have known who and what sort of woman this is who is touching him" (v.39). Jesus tells the parable of two debtors — one owed five hundred denarii, one fifty; the moneylender forgives both. "Which of them will love him more?" Simon correctly answers: the one forgiven more (vv.40–43). Jesus contrasts Simon's minimal hospitality with the woman's lavish devotion: no water for my feet / she wet them with tears; no kiss / she has kissed my feet; no anointing / she anointed them. "Her sins, which are many, are forgiven — for she loved much" (vv.44–47). To the woman: "Your sins are forgiven... Your faith has saved you; go in peace" (vv.48–50).
36.historical_context: This scene is unique to Luke and is sometimes confused with the Bethany anointing of Mark 14:3–9 / John 12:1–8 and with John 8's woman caught in adultery. These are distinct episodes: Luke's is in Galilee, during a Pharisee's dinner, at a different stage of the ministry. "A woman who was a sinner" (hē hamartolos) is almost certainly a recognized designation for a public prostitute. Anointing the feet rather than the head was an act of profound personal devotion — the head was the place of honor; the feet, the most servile. The alabaster flask suggests some wealth; the weeping suggests penitence. Simon's silent judgment ("if this man were a prophet") assumes that prophetic knowledge would include knowledge of this woman's status — and therefore Jesus would recoil from her touch.
36.literary_structure: The scene is a contrast study: Simon (named Pharisee, host, minimal hospitality, inward judgment) vs. the unnamed sinful woman (uninvited, extravagant hospitality, public devotion). The parable of the two debtors (vv.41–43) is Jesus's rhetorical tool for reframing Simon's judgment: the capacity for love corresponds to the awareness of forgiveness received. "She loved much because she was forgiven much" — the causal direction is debated (did she love because she was already forgiven, or was she forgiven because she loved?), but the Greek syntax (v.47: "her sins, which are many, are forgiven, hoti she loved much") most naturally reads the love as evidence of prior forgiveness, not the cause of it. The woman's love is the fruit of a forgiveness she already knew.
36.theological_application: "Your faith has saved you; go in peace" (v.50). The woman's faith — expressed entirely through the embodied acts of devotion (tears, hair, kisses, ointment) — is what saves her. She does not make a verbal confession, argue theology, or present credentials; she acts out of the overflow of gratitude for forgiveness. "Go in peace" (poreuou eis eirēnēn) is the dismissal formula of OT covenant blessing — the full Hebrew shalom: completeness, restoration, wholeness. The forgiveness Jesus pronounces is not merely the remission of guilt but the restoration of the person to wholeness. The Pharisees around the table ask "Who is this, who even forgives sins?" — the right question, to which the Gospel has been building the answer.
36.christology: "Who is this, who even forgives sins?" (v.49). The question is the Pharisees' — posed in the same episode where they have declined to receive him. Jesus pronounces forgiveness to the woman directly and personally: "Your sins are forgiven" and "Your faith has saved you" — both in the second-person singular, direct address. The claim to forgive sins here (as in the paralytic healing of 5:24) is a claim to do what only God does. The woman's extravagant love for Jesus is simultaneously a Christology of devotion — she treats him as the source of her restoration because he is. The contrast between Simon's minimal honor and her maximum devotion is the Gospel's question to every reader: how much have you understood of what has been forgiven?
